text.title: focus-groups Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for focus-groups as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: One loud voice asserting a position early, followed by apparent group agreement without independent confirmation.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Groupthink: participants anchor on the first articulate opinion and suppress dissent. Written-first exercises and direct probing of quiet participants are the only reliable countermeasures.

failure-mode.description: Treating a single group's consensus as representative of the segment.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: One group's "everyone agreed" is one group's dominant voice. Require at minimum 3 groups and cross-group comparison before any finding enters a decision artifact.

failure-mode.description: Moderator leading questions: "Don't you think this is confusing?" or "Most people find X helpful—do you agree?"
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Leading questions suppress genuine reaction and replace it with social compliance. All discussion guide questions must be neutral; run a lint pass on the guide before sessions.

failure-mode.description: Online focus groups running longer than 60 minutes or with more than 6 participants.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Online format loses body language, creates speaking-order awkwardness, and induces fatigue faster than in-person. Shorter sessions with smaller groups preserve data quality.
